examples.name: Kafka consumer groups
examples.note: Partitions are divided among the consumers in a group so exactly one member reads each partition; a rebalance reassigns partitions when a member joins or leaves. The partition count is the hard ceiling on parallelism — consumers beyond it sit idle with nothing assigned.

examples.name: Celery
examples.note: Python workers compete for tasks on a shared RabbitMQ or Redis broker, scaling by adding worker processes or raising --concurrency. worker_prefetch_multiplier controls how many tasks each worker reserves ahead of time, the knob that trades throughput against even distribution.

examples.name: Sidekiq
examples.note: Ruby workers compete for jobs on shared Redis queues, each process running a pool of threads (concurrency) that pull with BRPOP. Because plain BRPOP removes the job before it is done, an in-flight job is lost if the process is killed; reliable fetch that re-queues interrupted jobs is a Pro/Enterprise feature.

examples.name: Amazon SQS
examples.note: Many workers poll one queue and each message is handled once: a visibility timeout hides an in-flight message from sibling workers while it is processed, a Lambda event source mapping polls the queue and invokes functions in batches, and a redrive policy moves a message to a dead-letter queue after maxReceiveCount failed attempts.

fan-out.name: Fan-Out
fan-out.essence: One message in, an independent copy to every consumer
solves: one slow downstream holds up delivering the same event to everything else
shipping, email, and analytics all need the same order event at once and must not block each other
a consumer that was down loses every message published while it was offline
each service should handle the event at its own speed and retry on its own without touching the others
I do not want every service to receive every message, only the ones of a certain type

relations.note: Put a durable queue per consumer, each drained by a worker pool at its own pace.

themes.role: Dispatch parallel calls

examples.name: Amazon SNS → SQS fanout
examples.note: Publishing to an SNS topic pushes an independent copy to every subscribed SQS queue; each microservice owns a queue and drains it at its own pace, a Lambda event source mapping polls each queue in batches, subscription filter policies route by message attribute, and delivery failures land in a redrive dead-letter queue.

examples.name: Azure Service Bus topics
examples.note: A topic delivers each message into every subscription, and a subscription is its own durable queue-like buffer that a consumer reads independently; SQL and correlation filter rules decide which messages land in each subscription.

examples.name: Parallel LLM & agent fan-out
examples.note: An orchestrator dispatches one request to several models, tools, or sub-agents at once — running independent tool calls or sampling multiple generations in parallel so none waits on the others — and a fan-in gathers the results.

dead-letter-channel.name: Dead Letter Channel
dead-letter-channel.essence: Undeliverable messages land somewhere visible, not lost
solves: one malformed message keeps getting redelivered and nothing behind it ever moves
my consumer crash-loops on the same record forever and I have to purge the whole queue
messages that fail just vanish and we only find out weeks later from an angry customer
I have no idea which orders failed to process or why
a single record with a bad schema takes down the worker every time it restarts

examples.name: Amazon SQS redrive policy
examples.note: A source queue names a dead-letter queue and a maxReceiveCount in its redrive policy; once a message has been received that many times without being deleted, SQS moves it to the DLQ automatically. A redrive-to-source action moves messages back after the cause is fixed.

examples.name: RabbitMQ dead-letter exchanges
examples.note: The x-dead-letter-exchange argument (with an optional x-dead-letter-routing-key) republishes a message to a separate exchange when it is rejected with requeue=false, expires via message TTL, or exceeds the queue length limit — the three dead-letter triggers.

examples.name: Kafka Connect
examples.note: Setting errors.tolerance=all with errors.deadletterqueue.topic.name routes records a connector cannot convert or process to a dead-letter topic instead of failing the task; errors.deadletterqueue.context.headers.enable adds headers describing the failure to each dead-lettered record.

correlation-identifier.name: Correlation Identifier
correlation-identifier.essence: Tags related messages so replies can be matched up
solves: replies land on a shared channel and I cannot tell which one answers which request
I have to grep six services logs by timestamp to piece together what happened to one order
responses come back out of order and my client hands the wrong answer to the wrong caller
a customer reports a failure and I have no way to follow that one request across our services
I fired ten requests at once and their answers are interleaved with everyone elses

examples.name: W3C Trace Context
examples.note: Standardizes the traceparent header — version, 16-byte trace-id, 8-byte span-id, and flags — plus a tracestate header for vendor data, so every service in a call chain propagates one trace id that joins otherwise unrelated spans into a single trace.

examples.name: JMS JMSCorrelationID
examples.note: A standard JMS header the responder copies from the request into the reply so the requestor can match it against its pending calls; typically paired with JMSReplyTo, which names the queue the reply should return on.

examples.name: AMQP correlation_id
examples.note: A dedicated message property carried alongside reply_to; the RabbitMQ RPC tutorial documents using the two together to implement request/reply over queues, the responder echoing the correlation_id back on the reply-to queue.

scatter-gather.name: Scatter-Gather
scatter-gather.essence: Broadcasts a request, then merges the responses
solves: I call five providers one after another and the request takes ten seconds
every backend I add makes my endpoint another second slower
I want the cheapest quote but I have to ask everyone and I do not know who will bother to answer
one slow participant hangs the whole comparison and there is no way to move on without it
my caller should not have to know there are eight services behind this one call

examples.name: Elasticsearch
examples.note: A coordinating node scatters each search to every relevant shard in parallel, gathers the top hits from each, and merges them into one ranked result set; the two-phase query-then-fetch design is scatter-gather over shards.

examples.name: Apollo Federation
examples.note: The gateway compiles one client query into a query plan of parallel fetches against the owning subgraphs, then stitches their partial responses into a single result by entity keys.

wire-tap.name: Wire Tap
wire-tap.essence: Copies messages to a side channel for inspection, unchanged
solves: I need to see what is actually flowing through this channel and I cannot redeploy either end
adding logging to the consumer means editing code I do not own
I want to try the rewritten service against real traffic before trusting it with real requests
auditors want a record of everything that crossed this channel and nobody kept one

examples.name: Envoy request mirroring
examples.note: A route request_mirror_policies clause copies live traffic to a shadow cluster and discards the response, so the mirror cannot affect the real one; runtime_fraction samples what share of requests are mirrored, and the shadowed authority gets a -shadow suffix.

examples.name: nginx mirror module
examples.note: The ngx_http_mirror_module issues a background subrequest copy of each request to a mirror location and ignores the result; mirror_request_body controls whether the body is duplicated, and errors on the mirror path do not affect the original response.

examples.name: Apache Camel wireTap()
examples.note: Its wireTap() EIP sends a copy of the exchange to a second endpoint on a separate thread pool while the original route continues; the tap is InOnly and fire-and-forget, and an onPrepare processor can transform or redact the copy before it goes.

claim-check.name: Claim Check
claim-check.essence: Sends a reference to large data, not the data itself
solves: the broker rejects my message because the payload is over the size limit
I am pushing twenty megabyte files through a queue and the whole thing slowed to a crawl
my consumers only need the filename but every one of them downloads the entire blob
replaying the topic takes hours because every single record is enormous
our broker bill exploded because we are using it to move bulk data around

examples.name: Amazon SQS Extended Client Library
examples.note: Transparently stores any payload above the 256 KB SQS message limit in an S3 bucket and sends only the object pointer on the queue. payloadSizeThreshold sets the offload cutoff and alwaysThroughS3 forces every payload through S3; the receiving client fetches the object and can delete it after processing.

examples.name: Apache Camel claimCheck()
examples.note: The Claim Check EIP offers Set/Get/Push/Pop operations against a keyed repository: it moves the message body into the store under a claim-check key and replaces the exchange body with that key, then a later Get or Pop step restores the body on demand within the route.

idempotency.name: Idempotency
idempotency.essence: The same request applied twice has the same effect as once

my consumer picked up the same message after a crash and sent the confirmation email twice
I do not know whether the first attempt went through so I am scared to retry anything
double-clicking submit creates two orders and support has to clean it up by hand
replaying the queue after the outage duplicated every side effect downstream

examples.name: Stripe API
examples.note: Accepts an Idempotency-Key header on POST requests, stores the result of the first call, and replays the same response for any retry carrying the same key instead of charging a card twice. Reusing a key with different request parameters returns an error rather than a stale replay, and keys are retained for 24 hours, after which the same key is treated as new.

examples.name: HTTP methods (RFC 9110)
examples.note: Defines GET, HEAD, PUT, DELETE, OPTIONS, and TRACE as idempotent so intermediaries and clients may safely repeat them after a failed response; POST and PATCH are deliberately excluded, which is why write APIs bolt on an explicit idempotency key.

examples.name: Kafka idempotent producer
examples.note: With enable.idempotence=true, each batch is tagged with a producer id and a per-partition monotonic sequence number, so the broker discards duplicates from producer retries and preserves order within a single producer session.

someone fetched a record and forgot to put it back in memory, so it was slow forever and nobody noticed

examples.name: Caffeine (LoadingCache)
examples.note: Built from a CacheLoader, its LoadingCache computes absent entries inside get(), collapses concurrent misses for the same key into a single load, and exposes hit, miss, and load-failure counts once recordStats() is enabled.

examples.name: Guava CacheLoader
examples.note: A CacheBuilder plus a CacheLoader yields a LoadingCache whose get() loads and populates missing entries transparently; getAll can batch-load, and expireAfterWrite and maximumSize bound each entry.

examples.name: Apache Kafka producer
examples.note: Accumulates records per partition and sends them together; batch.size caps bytes per batch and linger.ms adds a small wait so more records join each send.

examples.name: TCP Nagle's algorithm
examples.note: Coalesces small outgoing TCP segments until an ack returns or enough data accumulates, trading latency for fewer packets; TCP_NODELAY turns it off.

examples.name: OpenAI Batch API
examples.note: Submit a file of requests to run asynchronously (within 24h) at lower cost than synchronous calls — trading latency for throughput and price.

examples.name: vLLM continuous batching
examples.note: An LLM serving engine that adds and evicts requests from the running GPU batch at each decoding step, keeping the batch full instead of waiting for the slowest request to finish.

write-through.name: Write-Through
write-through.essence: Writes hit the cache and the store together, synchronously
solves: a user saves a change, the page reloads, and the old value comes back

examples.name: jMock

examples.name: EasyMock
examples.note: Records expected calls during a setup phase, switches to replay with `replay()`, and `verify()` fails the test if the actual calls did not match the recorded script.

examples.name: Mockito
examples.note: Generates doubles at runtime; `verify(mock, times(n))` checks interactions after the fact and names the exact call that was missing or malformed, and strict stubbing flags expectations that were set but never used.

fake-object.name: Fake Object
fake-object.essence: A working lightweight stand-in, like an in-memory database
solves: my tests need a real database running and ci keeps timing out waiting for it

examples.name: LocalStack
examples.note: Emulates AWS service APIs — S3, SQS, DynamoDB, Lambda and more — in a local container; the SDK points at its endpoint URL instead of the real AWS endpoint, so the same client code runs unchanged.

examples.name: Firebase Local Emulator Suite
examples.note: Officially maintained emulators for Firestore, Authentication, Functions and the Realtime Database, started with `firebase emulators:start`; client SDKs connect to the local host and port, and emulator state can be imported and exported between runs.

examples.name: pyfakefs
examples.note: Replaces the standard os, io and pathlib calls with a fake filesystem; drops in as the pytest `fs` fixture or via patchfs, so file-handling code runs unchanged and leaves nothing on disk.

examples.name: fakeredis
examples.note: Implements a large subset of the Redis command set in-process, acting as a drop-in for the redis-py client so code using real Redis operations runs without a redis-server.

page-object.name: Page Object
page-object.essence: Wraps a UI screen's structure behind one testable interface

examples.name: Selenium PageFactory
examples.note: `PageFactory.initElements(driver, page)` populates fields annotated with `@FindBy` locators as lazy proxies resolved on first use; `AjaxElementLocatorFactory` wraps each with an implicit wait, keeping selectors in the page object rather than the test.

examples.name: Playwright
examples.note: Its official Page Object Model guide builds page classes that wrap auto-waiting `Locator`s behind named actions, and custom test fixtures inject those page objects into each test.

test-data-builder.name: Test Data Builder
test-data-builder.essence: Fluent construction of valid objects for test setup

examples.note: The Ruby fixture library; each factory defines a valid default with a `factory` block, a test overrides only the attributes its scenario needs, and traits and associations compose whole object graphs.

examples.name: factory_boy
examples.note: Brings defaults-plus-overrides fixture construction to Python; `SubFactory` builds nested objects and Faker or sequences fill fields, so a test states only what differs from a valid default.

golden-master.essence: Compares fresh output against a saved known-good snapshot

examples.name: Jest snapshot testing
examples.note: Writes a component or value to a `.snap` file under __snapshots__ on first run and fails later runs on any diff; `--update-snapshot` blesses changes, `--ci` refuses to write new snapshots so a forgotten one fails, and property matchers like `expect.any()` cover nondeterministic fields.

examples.name: ApprovalTests
examples.note: On mismatch it launches a configured diff reporter and you approve by promoting the received file to the approved file; scrubbers normalize nondeterministic content, and the library ships for Java, .NET, Python and other languages.

examples.name: insta
examples.note: The Rust snapshot library; `cargo insta review` walks each changed snapshot for an accept-or-reject verdict, the INSTA_UPDATE environment variable controls update mode, and snapshots can be stored inline in the source file.

examples.name: Chromatic
examples.note: Captures Storybook stories and pixel-diffs each against a per-branch baseline, requiring human sign-off on every visual change; TurboSnap skips stories unaffected by the git diff to cut the comparison set.

examples.name: Spring Security

examples.name: Django AuthenticationMiddleware
examples.note: Paired with SessionMiddleware, it lazily resolves the session key into request.user on every request via the configured auth backend, so views trust request.user instead of verifying credentials themselves.

examples.name: Envoy ext_authz filter
examples.note: An HTTP filter that calls an external authorization service (gRPC or HTTP) for each proxied request and forwards only the verified identity upstream. Its failure_mode_allow flag makes the fail-open/fail-closed choice an explicit configuration decision.

examples.name: Open Policy Agent
examples.note: A general-purpose policy decision point: applications ask OPA whether an action is allowed and it evaluates Rego policies against the request. Policy and data are distributed as bundles the agent polls on an interval, and every decision can be emitted to a decision log for audit.

examples.name: Kubernetes RBAC
examples.note: Role and ClusterRole objects define permissions; RoleBinding and ClusterRoleBinding attach them to subjects. With the RBAC authorization mode enabled, the API server checks every request against the bindings and denies by default — a subject with no binding can do nothing.

examples.name: AWS IAM
examples.note: Every API call is evaluated against attached identity-based and resource-based policies in one central decision point. Access is denied by default, an explicit Deny always overrides an Allow, and the effective decision is the combined result of all applicable policies.

secure-session-manager.name: Secure Session Manager
secure-session-manager.essence: Issues, tracks, and expires session state safely

examples.name: Django session framework
examples.note: Stores a random session key in the cookie while keeping session data in the backend chosen by SESSION_ENGINE (database, cache, or signed cookie). SESSION_COOKIE_HTTPONLY defaults to on, SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE and SESSION_COOKIE_AGE are configurable, and the login flow cycles the session key to defeat fixation.

examples.name: Spring Session
examples.note: Replaces the container HttpSession with an implementation backed by a SessionRepository, with Redis, JDBC, and Hazelcast backends, so any application instance in a cluster can validate, look up, and revoke the same session independently of which node created it.

examples.name: express-session
examples.note: Middleware that stores a session ID in a cookie signed with a configured secret and keeps state in a pluggable store (connect-redis and others). Cookie options cover httpOnly, secure, sameSite, and maxAge, and req.session.regenerate() rotates the ID on demand.

examples.name: Pydantic in FastAPI
examples.note: FastAPI parses each request body into a declared Pydantic model before the endpoint function runs; a mismatch raises a RequestValidationError that returns a 422 with per-field error detail. A model config of extra=forbid turns unknown fields into a rejection.

examples.name: Hibernate Validator (Bean Validation)
examples.note: The reference implementation of Jakarta Bean Validation. Constraint annotations (@NotNull, @Size, @Pattern) declared on request objects are checked at the controller boundary when the argument is marked @Valid, before the method body executes.

examples.name: express-validator
examples.note: Built on validator.js, it composes body()/check() validation and sanitization rules into an Express middleware chain that runs ahead of the route handler; accumulated errors are read back with validationResult(req) to reject the request.

examples.note: Records account API activity as an append-only trail delivered to S3. Its log file integrity validation produces hourly digest files containing SHA-256 hashes of the delivered logs, signed so that any modification or deletion of a log file after the fact is detectable.

examples.name: Pino (redact option)
examples.note: Backed by fast-redact, it takes a configured list of field paths in dot and bracket notation and replaces their values with a censor placeholder (or removes them) as the log line is serialized, so the secret never reaches the sink.

examples.name: Linux auditd
examples.note: The userspace daemon for the kernel audit subsystem: rules loaded via auditctl funnel security-relevant kernel events into an access-controlled log at /var/log/audit/audit.log, which ausearch and aureport then query for forensic review.

Paired with a Tunnel, the origin accepts traffic only from Cloudflare and never listens on the public internet.

examples.name: Teleport
examples.note: A single hardened access proxy that all SSH, Kubernetes, database, and web-app traffic to a protected fleet must pass through. It authenticates against an IdP, issues short-lived certificates instead of standing credentials, and records every session for audit.

examples.name: Kong Gateway
examples.note: Built on Nginx/OpenResty, it fronts a fleet of upstream services as one entry point and applies authentication, rate-limiting, and logging through plugins (key-auth, jwt, rate-limiting) before routing each request to its backend.

examples.name: OpenBSD pledge
examples.note: A process calls pledge(2) to voluntarily restrict itself to a named set of promises (stdio, rpath, inet, ...); once pledged it cannot widen the set, and any syscall outside it is terminated with SIGABRT. Its companion unveil(2) does the same for filesystem paths.

examples.name: Linux capabilities
examples.note: The monolithic power of root is split into discrete units (capabilities(7)); a process granted only CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE can bind a port below 1024 without holding any other superuser power, so a daemon runs with a fraction of full privilege.

examples.name: AWS STS temporary credentials
examples.note: AssumeRole mints short-lived credentials with an explicit session duration (DurationSeconds, from 15 minutes up to 12 hours) in place of long-lived access keys, and an inline session policy can further narrow the assumed role to only what the current task needs.

monostate.name: Monostate
monostate.essence: Many instances, all sharing one state
solves: I want every instance of my config class to read and write the same values
callers already do new everywhere and I need them all to share one state
changing a setting on one object should change it for all the others
I need one shared instance but without a global getInstance access point

relations.note: Both yield one shared state — Monostate hides it behind ordinary instances, Singleton behind one global object.

examples.name: Borg idiom (Python)
examples.note: Alex Martelli's recipe, popularised through the Python Cookbook: every new instance rebinds its own __dict__ to one class-level dict (self.__dict__ = self._shared_state), so all instances are distinct objects that transparently share every attribute. It is Python's canonical name for Monostate — and the reason this pattern's alias is "Borg."

token-bucket.name: Token Bucket
token-bucket.essence: Caps the average rate but lets short bursts through
solves: one client is hammering my API and starving everyone else
I need to allow short bursts of traffic but cap the sustained rate
my fixed-window counter lets double the requests through at the window boundary
a flood of requests is overwhelming my backend and I need to throttle callers

relations.note: Token Bucket is the classic rate-limiting algorithm — it permits bursts up to the bucket size, then paces to the refill rate.

examples.name: Envoy
examples.note: Its local rate-limit filter is configured with a literal token_bucket block — max_tokens (capacity), tokens_per_fill, and fill_interval — spending a token per matching request.

examples.name: Go golang.org/x/time/rate
examples.note: The standard rate.Limiter is a token bucket: NewLimiter(r, b) sets the refill rate r and burst size b, exposed through Allow, Wait, and Reserve.

examples.name: Guava RateLimiter
examples.note: Hands out permits at a fixed rate and lets unused permits accumulate up to a small burst (SmoothBursty) — token-bucket behaviour under the hood.

examples.name: AWS API Gateway
examples.note: Request throttling uses the token bucket algorithm, with a steady-state rateLimit (refill) and a burstLimit (bucket capacity) per stage or route.

reverse-proxy.name: Reverse Proxy
reverse-proxy.essence: Fronts hidden backends, forwarding and terminating client connections for them
solves: our app servers are exposed straight to the internet and I want a single guarded entry point in front of them
I need to add HTTPS in front of several backend services without changing TLS in each one
I want one domain to serve several apps and forward each URL path to a different backend
clients need to reach services that sit on a private network they can't address directly
every backend re-implements TLS, gzip and caching and I want that handled once at the front

relations.note: A reverse proxy forwards and terminates connections; an API gateway adds routing, auth, rate limiting and aggregation on top of that role.

examples.name: NGINX
examples.note: The proxy_pass directive forwards requests to an upstream backend; NGINX terminates TLS, can cache responses with proxy_cache, and rewrites headers so the backend never faces the client directly.

examples.name: Caddy
examples.note: Its reverse_proxy directive proxies to backends and, as its headline feature, provisions and renews TLS certificates automatically over ACME — so the front door is HTTPS by default with no manual certificate handling.

examples.name: Traefik
examples.note: An edge router that discovers backends dynamically from Docker, Kubernetes and other providers, wiring up routes and TLS as containers come and go instead of requiring a static config reload.

service-mesh.name: Service Mesh
service-mesh.essence: Moves service-to-service networking into a managed proxy layer

examples.name: Istio
examples.note: The most widely deployed mesh; an Envoy sidecar in every pod forms the data plane while the istiod control plane distributes routing, policy, and workload certificates. Provides mutual TLS, traffic splitting, and fault injection, and also offers a sidecarless ambient mode.

examples.name: Linkerd
examples.note: A CNCF-graduated mesh that uses its own lightweight Rust micro-proxy (linkerd2-proxy) rather than Envoy; mutual TLS between meshed services is on by default, and it exposes golden metrics per service.

examples.name: Consul
examples.note: HashiCorp Consul pairs its service catalog with Envoy sidecars to provide mutual TLS and intention-based authorization between services across VMs and Kubernetes.

examples.name: Cilium
examples.note: Uses eBPF in the Linux kernel to deliver mesh features — identity-based policy, mutual TLS, and Hubble observability — with a node-level data path rather than a per-pod sidecar.

sticky-session.name: Sticky Session

examples.name: HAProxy

examples.name: NGINX
examples.note: Open-source NGINX pins by client address with ip_hash in an upstream block; NGINX Plus adds cookie-based affinity via sticky cookie (plus sticky route and sticky learn) so the pin survives a client changing IP.

examples.name: AWS Elastic Load Balancing
examples.note: Application Load Balancer target-group stickiness pins a client for a configurable duration using a load-balancer-generated cookie (AWSALB) or an application-defined cookie, keeping a session on one target.

examples.name: Traefik
examples.note: A service load balancer can enable sticky sessions with a cookie whose name, Secure, HttpOnly and SameSite attributes are configurable, pinning a client to the same server.

stateless-service.name: Stateless Service

examples.name: AWS Lambda

examples.name: Kubernetes
examples.note: A Deployment manages interchangeable, fungible replica Pods for stateless workloads, in contrast to a StatefulSet, which grants Pods stable identities and persistent storage for stateful ones.

examples.name: REST
examples.note: Roy Fielding's REST style makes statelessness an architectural constraint: each request must carry all the information needed to understand it, with session state kept on the client rather than the server.

two constants encode the same fact and they've drifted out of sync

kiss.name: Keep It Simple (KISS)
kiss.essence: Prefer the simplest design that actually meets the requirement
solves: this simple feature turned into a maze of abstractions nobody can follow

every trivial change has to thread through five layers of indirection
the solution is cleverer than the problem and nobody can debug it
